key_insight: AI is shifting nanoparticle electron microscopy from image interpretation to scientific inference by extracting structure, dynamics, and property-relevant information from increasingly complex datasets.
engineering_relevance: For materials engineers, these methods can speed up nanoparticle characterization, improve defect and morphology analysis, and support closed-loop discovery workflows in TEM/STEM.
method: Conventional ML, CNNs, and transformer-based vision models for detection, segmentation, restoration, and defect identification
Self-supervised, foundation, multimodal, and physics-informed learning to leverage limited labels and fuse images with metadata/simulations
AI-enabled in situ analysis and autonomous experimentation to connect synthesis conditions, structure evolution, and function
evidence_signal: The review synthesizes methods across TEM, HRTEM, STEM, and in situ TEM, and critically assesses their advantages, limitations, benchmarking, and data requirements.
watch_item: Performance and generalization depend strongly on high-quality benchmarks, sufficient training data, and careful validation across microscopes, sample types, and imaging conditions.
paper_title: The evolution of AI from image interpretation toward scientific inference in nanoparticle electron microscopy
